decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)

requirements.text: Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing target architecture and platform capabilities.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing operating model and support framework.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing commercial and pricing structure.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
The evaluation should ensure that application performance and availability are maintained or improved.
The evaluation should ensure that integration with manufacturing and enterprise systems is supported.
The evaluation should ensure that transition risks and business disruption are minimized.
Vendors must use detailed requirements, response structure, and evaluation inputs provided in the Evaluation Template Excel shared as part of the RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Detailed requirements, response structure, and evaluation inputs are stated to be in an external Evaluation Template Excel that is not included in the visible RFP.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is a common disqualifier area; this RFP explicitly requires a data residency approach but does not state a specific residency restriction in the visible document.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach are required; no specific data location or sovereignty restriction is stated in the visible RFP.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, including Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid, where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.
In-scope solution coverage includes enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
In-scope solution coverage includes commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications.
In-scope solution coverage includes Windows and Linux virtual machines.
In-scope solution coverage includes databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL and other supported platforms.
In-scope solution coverage includes middleware and integration platforms.
In-scope solution coverage includes file storage systems and shared infrastructure services.
In-scope solution coverage includes backup and disaster recovery solutions.
In-scope solution coverage includes identity and access management integrations.
In-scope solution coverage includes network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments.
In-scope solution coverage includes monitoring, logging, and security tooling.
In-scope solution coverage includes selected OT-adjacent workloads, limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only under limited read-only or controlled integration scenarios; this scope qualifier can be missed in the in-scope services list.

requirements.text: Vendors must use the detailed inventory of in-scope applications and infrastructure attached in the RFP package online to support response, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The baseline inventory is referenced as an attached online RFP package artifact; it is not included in the visible document but is required for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.

requirements.text: Detailed migration execution and implementation services, including actual workload movement, cutover planning, and execution activities, are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The out-of-scope list materially constrains the expected proposal; including execution commitments as base scope may misalign the proposal.

requirements.text: Application refactoring, remediation, or modernization activities, including code changes, re-platforming, and re-architecting, are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The out-of-scope list materially constrains the expected proposal; application modernization activities are excluded from this Phase 0 scope.

requirements.text: Detailed low-level design and configuration-level architecture beyond high-level solution design and reference architecture are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP seeks high-level design only; low-level design and configuration-level architecture are explicitly out of scope.

requirements.text: Operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, including full-scale IT operations and support models, are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Steady-state managed services delivery commitments are explicitly out of scope despite operating model and support capabilities being evaluation topics.

requirements.text: Execution of security controls, compliance certification, or regulatory audits is out of scope; vendors are only required to demonstrate capability and readiness.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP asks vendors to demonstrate compliance capability/readiness, but execution of controls, certifications, or audits is excluded from this phase.

requirements.text: Tool implementation, automation deployment, or integration development is out of scope unless required to illustrate proposed solution capability.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Tool implementation and automation deployment are excluded unless needed only to illustrate proposed capability.

requirements.text: End-user training, change management execution, and organizational transformation activities are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
Data cleansing, data transformation, or archival activities beyond indicative approaches are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
Detailed testing execution and validation activities, including UAT, SIT, or performance testing, are out of scope; only approach and methodology are expected.
Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, and evidence of certifications is required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Certification evidence is explicitly required, but the named certifications and applicable standards are not visible in this document.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications are not identified in the visible RFP.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a provider-type-specific compliance obligation and the RFP points to the Vendor response template for more information, which is not included in the visible document.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Industry standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to review, validate, and clearly state any deviations or additional assumptions in their proposal.
Vendors are expected to propose Cloud, DCaaS, and/or Hybrid hosting solutions aligned to Kohler’s stated objectives and constraints.
Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The provided inventory dataset is a mandatory response baseline but is not included in the visible document.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Vendors must actively identify data gaps and assumptions; failure to do so may make sizing/costing non-comparable.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory dataset is a required baseline for sizing and pricing but is external to the visible RFP.

requirements.text: Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Vendor responses are expected to be structured, complete, and comparable, aligned to the requirements in the RFP and associated response templates.
Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission must be made within the defined timeline and via designated channel; the RFP does not explicitly say late or misdirected submissions are rejected, but this is a critical compliance instruction.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP explicitly states that simple Yes/No answers are insufficient, so narrative/detail is required in the template response.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the Q&A template provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification questions have a fixed deadline and must use a provided Q&A template.

requirements.text: All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of the RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Use of the Vendor Response template is mandatory, but the template is external to the visible document.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured, and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of the RFP and the inventory baseline provided.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including foundational setup costs such as landing zone, security, governance, and compliance configurations.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including application and infrastructure hosting costs for compute, storage, networking, and platform services.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including migration or implementation costs, if applicable, including transition or onboarding activities.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency compliance is a strict pricing instruction; all commercial values must be in USD.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: United States Dollars required for pricing submissions.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Pricing must address cost protections and efficiency gains over time, and reductions should be based on prices paid rather than list-price discounts.

requirements.text: Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 is not labeled as an absolute disqualifier, but the RFP states anything less will not be considered competitive.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed.
The governance overview should include an indicative governance structure and engagement model.
The governance overview should include reporting and review mechanisms, such as periodic status updates and performance tracking.
The governance overview should include an escalation framework for issue resolution.
The governance overview should include key roles and responsibilities at a high level.
All information shared as part of this RFP is confidential and must be treated accordingly by participating vendors.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality obligations are in the appendix and can be missed; breach may create legal exposure.

requirements.text: Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Use limitation appears in the appendix and restricts use of Kohler information solely to the RFP response.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Third-party disclosure restriction is in the appendix; this may affect subcontractor, hyperscaler, advisor, or partner involvement unless prior written consent is obtained.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Appendix compliance language requires capability to comply with standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations, but specifics are deferred/not listed.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments are referenced but not specified.

ambiguities: The visible RFP contains no explicit auto-elimination language such as mandatory rejection for noncompliance; therefore eliminatory_count is 0 and hard_disqualifiers is empty to avoid inferring unstated disqualifiers.
Key dates in the timeline list day/month only and omit the year; the cover page date is 29-05-2026, but the timeline table itself does not explicitly repeat 2026.

Detailed requirements, response structure, evaluation inputs, scoring structure, and weightages are stated to be in the Evaluation Template (Excel), which is referenced but not included in the provided document.
The Vendor Response template is mandatory and referenced for additional information, including Section 4 compliance details, but the template itself is not included in the provided document.
The inventory baseline dataset is referenced as attached/provided online but is not included in the provided document; sizing and pricing requirements may depend on that dataset.

The RFP requires evidence of certifications and compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, but it does not list the specific certifications, standards, jurisdictions, or regulatory regimes required.
The RFP states DCaaS providers must provide compliance attestations as per industry standards, but does not specify which attestations or standards are required.
Payment terms shorter than net 90 are stated to be not competitive, but the RFP does not explicitly state that such proposals will be rejected or disqualified.
The RFP requires submission within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel, but it does not specify whether late submissions are automatically rejected.

Detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, service-level agreements, and service delivery frameworks will be defined in later phases, so final contractual risk is not fully visible in this document.
compliance_red_flags: No explicit hard disqualifiers are stated in the visible document, but multiple mandatory response instructions exist and may affect compliance if not followed.
Vendors must provide evidence of certifications for applicable industry/regional standards, but the specific certifications are not listed in the RFP text. [PAGE 7]
DCaaS providers have an additional explicit obligation to provide compliance attestations as per industry standards, with further detail referenced in the Vendor response template. [PAGE 7]
All responses must use the provided Vendor Response template; the template is not included in the visible document. [PAGE 9]
Detailed requirements, evaluation inputs, scoring, and weightages are in the Evaluation Template Excel, which is not included in the visible document. [PAGE 6], [PAGE 10], [PAGE 12]
All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in USD. [PAGE 9]
Anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and proposals should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms. [PAGE 10]
Kohler information must be used solely for responding to the RFP and must not be disclosed, distributed, or shared with third parties without prior written consent. [PAGE 11]
Kohler reserves the right to modify, amend, or withdraw the RFP; request additional information; and accept or reject any proposal without obligation or liability. [PAGE 11]
Data residency must be addressed as part of the solution, but the RFP does not state specific data residency restrictions or prohibited locations in the visible document. [PAGE 6]

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 48%. Positive evidence: there is a direct Project Polaris proposal record (RFP-3E0EFE) with very high relevance and a reusable 12-week fixed-price Hosting Decision Lab priced at $279,533.42; the approach maps well to Kohler’s must-have areas of target architecture, transition sequencing, operating model, support framework, commercial transparency, hybrid hosting, resiliency, FinOps, and workload placement. The deal corpus also shows multiple manufacturing transformation wins: DEAL-003 won on global manufacturing proof, zero-downtime methodology, and follow-the-sun delivery; DEAL-089 won on rapid mobilisation; DEAL-038 and DEAL-094 won where proprietary IP reduced transition risk. Negative evidence: no public procurement award data is available, so competitor pricing and incumbent win patterns cannot be validated; the direct Kohler record is WIP/unknown outcome, not a proven win; the closest corpus wins are broader manufacturing IT transformations rather than exact cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting advisory awards; the Evaluation Template Excel is not available in the provided intelligence; and historical losses show material risks around missed compliance appendices (DEAL-056) and overpricing despite better technical quality (DEAL-037). Because procurement ground truth is absent and evidence of exact-scope wins is limited, the probability is kept below 50% despite strong solution fit. Confidence: medium-low, based on two useful intelligence layers plus no public procurement validation.
capability_gaps: Requirement: use the detailed Evaluation Template Excel. Gap: the available proposal intelligence explicitly notes the exact evaluation weights/details are in an external Excel template not included in the supplied text, creating a compliance and scoring-risk gap until every template field is completed and cross-checked.

Requirement: clearly articulate target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles. Gap: the past proposal supports a hybrid landing-zone blueprint and guardrails, but the intelligence does not evidence a fully detailed account/subscription hierarchy, management group structure, policy inheritance model, naming/tagging standards, or platform-specific landing-zone design.

Requirement: clearly articulate landing-zone deployment model and application deployment model. Gap: the prior content is strong at advisory/decision-lab level but does not evidence detailed deployment patterns by workload class, CI/CD model, infrastructure-as-code approach, or application onboarding factory.

Requirement: clearly articulate identity integration with enterprise identity providers. Gap: the proposal mentions identity, security, and governance guardrails, but the intelligence does not show a detailed Kohler-specific identity integration approach, federation model, privileged access model, or break-glass design.

Requirement: clearly articulate hybrid connectivity approach. Gap: the available proposal content references networking and connectivity design, but does not evidence detailed WAN/cloud interconnect patterns, plant/edge connectivity, routing segmentation, bandwidth assumptions, latency targets, or Wisconsin data center connectivity design.

Requirement: clearly articulate region strategy and data residency approach. Gap: the proposal references global/multiple regions and data-center/cloud evaluation, but the intelligence does not show a specific region selection framework, US/Wisconsin data residency position, DR-region restrictions, or workload-level data classification mapping.

Requirement: commercial and pricing structure. Gap: the past proposal provides a 12-week fixed-price advisory anchor of $279,533.42 and USD-only open-book TCO, but does not evidence detailed downstream hosting/DCaaS/cloud managed-service unit economics, transition charges, consumption assumptions, or benchmarked competitiveness against market awards.
recommended_win_themes: Evidence-led hybrid hosting decision for a global manufacturer: position Polaris as a resilience, cost, architecture, and operating-model decision rather than a binary cloud-versus-data-center migration, reusing the directly relevant Kohler Hosting Decision Lab framing.

Low-disruption manufacturing transition: emphasize workload-by-workload placement, dependency mapping, transition sequencing, rollback gates, zero/low-downtime methods, and protection of plant, enterprise, hospitality, analytics, and digital workloads.

Secure enterprise landing zone and hybrid connectivity: lead with multi-account/multi-subscription landing zones, identity integration, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, policy-as-code, region/data residency strategy, secure connectivity, and integrated observability.

Global operating model with fast mobilisation: use the DEAL-003 and DEAL-089 lessons by showing follow-the-sun support, clear RACI, vendor coexistence with incumbent providers, escalation governance, and a rapid mobilisation plan.

Transparent commercial decision package: anchor the advisory at the known $279,533.42 / 12-week fixed-price decision-lab structure while adding open-book USD-only TCO, scenario economics, FinOps guardrails, and downstream run-cost transparency.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so there is no ground-truth validation of recent winning prices, incumbent strength, or competitor win patterns for comparable Kohler/hosting advisory work.
The most relevant past proposal is the same Kohler Project Polaris WIP record, not an evidenced win; it proves relevance of solution framing but not buyer acceptance.
The RFP depends on an Evaluation Template Excel that is not available in the supplied intelligence; missed fields or buried compliance requirements could be disqualifying or materially reduce score.
The deal corpus contains strong manufacturing transformation wins, but none is an exact match for cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting evaluation at Kohler; similarity is directional rather than definitive.

Commercial competitiveness is unvalidated. DEAL-037 shows that a better technical proposal can still lose if pricing is 20% above the winning bid, and there is no procurement benchmark to confirm the $279,533.42 advisory anchor is market-winning.
Potential incumbent or competitor advantage is unknown. The past proposal references a Cognizant/vendor coexistence model, suggesting existing provider complexity may influence evaluation.

The current evidence supports an advisory/decision-lab offer more strongly than a full downstream hosting/managed-services commercial response; if Kohler expects implementation-ready pricing, the current package may be underspecified.

Manufacturing infrastructure opportunities are sensitive to local entity, compliance, data residency, and operational continuity requirements; DEAL-056 demonstrates that a single missed compliance appendix item can cause a loss.
must_fix_before_bidding: Obtain and complete the Evaluation Template Excel line-by-line; perform an independent compliance review to avoid the DEAL-056 pattern where a manufacturing opportunity was lost due to a missed appendix/local-entity requirement.

Convert the 12-week Hosting Decision Lab concept into a requirement-mapped response with explicit sections for target architecture, transition sequencing, operating model, support framework, and commercial structure.

Add a provider-specific and/or provider-neutral landing-zone design covering account/subscription hierarchy, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, IAM, network segmentation, logging, backup, DR, policy-as-code, tagging, FinOps, and security baselines.

Build a detailed hybrid connectivity and manufacturing integration narrative covering Wisconsin data center, plant/edge environments, enterprise systems, latency-sensitive workloads, ERP/manufacturing dependencies, and secure connectivity patterns.

Strengthen the commercial model beyond the $279,533.42 advisory price: include transparent assumptions, pricing options by hosting scenario, TCO methodology, transition pricing, run-cost model, and value/cost-takeout levers to avoid the DEAL-037 loss pattern of being technically strong but overpriced.

Include concrete mobilisation evidence: named roles or role profiles, Day 0-Day 30/60/90 plan, governance cadence, discovery data requests, client dependencies, and rapid-start commitments aligned to the DEAL-089 win pattern.
Validate US legal entity, Wisconsin data center, data residency, security certification, subcontractor, insurance, disaster recovery, and any state/local procurement conditions before submission.

Weak commercial transparency: no open-book TCO, unclear assumptions, no licensing treatment, no FinOps model, or pricing that cannot be compared across scenarios.
Ignoring incumbent/vendor coexistence; Kohler is likely to favor a low-disruption path that works with current providers.
Providing insufficient evidence for compliance, certifications, security operations, DR/HA, and data residency.
Missing mandatory response-template instructions or not using the Evaluation Template Excel exactly as requested.
Treating the Wisconsin data center as a simple data-center exit when the real decision is operating-model, resilience, risk, and cost optimization for a global enterprise.
Overcommitting to migration outcomes without having the inventory baseline, dependencies, contractual constraints, or application criticality data.
Losing to an incumbent or larger global SI because they appear lower-risk and already understand the estate.

draft.rfp_id: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
draft.executive_summary:
Kohler’s Project Polaris is not a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise; it is a resilience, cost, architecture, and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer whose enterprise platforms, Wisconsin Data Center, plants, hospitality, analytics, connected products, and customer digital experiences must continue to perform while the target state is evaluated. Kohler’s Chief Digital Officer, Eric Tabor, has publicly emphasized using technology to improve operational and business excellence, productivity, responsiveness to business needs, and customer value. Our proposal translates that direction into a vendor-neutral, evidence-led Hosting Decision Lab that gives Kohler a defensible path across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration options.

HCLTech will deliver a 12-week, fixed-price Polaris decision package for $279,533, with open-book USD-only TCO, FinOps guardrails, security and landing-zone blueprints, transition sequencing, rollback gates, and a clear vendor-coexistence RACI. We will stand up a named onsite Wisconsin Data Center transition cell and place 20% of fees at risk against maintaining or improving application performance and availability during discovery and transition planning, with zero unplanned manufacturing disruption attributable to our evaluation work.
win_themes: Evidence-led hybrid hosting decision for a global manufacturer
Secure enterprise landing zone and hybrid connectivity
Global operating model with fast mobilisation

sections.content:
Polaris sits at the intersection of infrastructure sourcing, ERP modernization, cybersecurity, and global manufacturing continuity. Kohler must compare cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin Data Center, hybrid, edge/plant, and SaaS models without disrupting SAP ECC modernization dependencies, 25+ enterprise-process harmonization, plant connectivity, hospitality systems, analytics, AI, or connected-product services. The immediate question is not “where can servers run cheapest?” It is which hosting pattern gives each workload the right resilience, latency, security, licensing position, support model, and measurable run cost.

We understand Kohler’s multi-partner environment and the need to coexist with incumbent providers, including advanced cloud management, data-center operations, cybersecurity, and observability responsibilities already in motion. The evaluation must therefore produce auditable evidence, not opinions: workload dependencies, service levels, contracts, cost baselines, regulatory and data-residency considerations, uptime and MTTR targets, and vendor accountability. Our approach treats Polaris as a controlled operating-model decision that protects manufacturing while enabling a governed cloud and DCaaS future.

HCLTech proposes a balanced, vendor-neutral 12-week Polaris Hosting Decision Lab delivered for a fixed price of $279,533. The lab will baseline current workloads, dependencies, costs, service levels, security controls, contracts, and operational metrics, then evaluate public cloud, DCaaS/colocation, retained Wisconsin Data Center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration options using common placement criteria. The result is a Kohler-owned decision package aligned to the Evaluation Template Excel once supplied.

The solution includes a multi-account and multi-subscription landing-zone blueprint; production, test, and development segregation; identity and privileged-access integration; policy-as-code guardrails; region and data-residency strategy; secure connectivity between Wisconsin, global operations, plants, cloud, and DCaaS; and integrated observability for uptime, MTTR, SLA, and escalation visibility. We will add open-book TCO and FinOps analysis covering run cost, migration cost, licensing, service levels, chargeback/showback, and vendor accountability.

Our differentiator is manufacturing continuity with commercial accountability: a named onsite Wisconsin Data Center transition cell and 20% of fees at risk unless application performance and availability are maintained or improved during evaluation activity, with zero unplanned manufacturing disruption attributable to our work. This creates a decision package Kohler can execute confidently.

Our delivery method follows the winning Polaris pattern: mobilize, baseline, benchmark, architect, and decide. In Weeks 1–3, we mobilize governance, confirm scope, establish the onsite Wisconsin Data Center transition cell, collect workload and cost data, and build the current-state inventory, dependency map, data-request tracker, and initial risk register. Manufacturing continuity gates are established before any discovery activity that could affect production.

In Weeks 4–6, we run workload-placement and benchmarking workshops for representative enterprise, SAP/ERP, manufacturing, hospitality, analytics, AI, digital, connected-product, and plant/edge workloads. Each workload is scored against business criticality, latency, resilience, DR, security, compliance, data residency, licensing, operational maturity, and TCO.

In Weeks 7–9, we define target-state architecture: landing zones, identity, secure connectivity, region strategy, observability, security operations, resilience tiers, backup and DR patterns, FinOps guardrails, and service-integration requirements. Coexistence with incumbent providers is designed into the RACI, tooling integration, and escalation model.

In Weeks 10–12, we produce the leadership-ready decision package: cloud/DCaaS/hybrid disposition by workload, transition waves, rollback gates, risk controls, KPI baseline, TCO scenarios, and implementation roadmap. Every phase ends with evidence review and decision checkpoints, keeping Polaris low-disruption, commercially transparent, and ready for governed execution.

HCLTech will staff Polaris with a senior, manufacturing-aware advisory team built for fast mobilisation and vendor coexistence. The engagement lead will own executive governance, decision quality, and alignment to Kohler’s Evaluation Template. The hybrid-architecture lead will cover public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, plant/edge, SaaS integration, secure connectivity, and landing-zone design. Security and identity specialists will define policy-as-code, privileged access, data-residency, cybersecurity, and observability requirements. FinOps and commercial analysts will produce the open-book USD TCO model, licensing view, SLA/KPI baseline, and downstream run-cost scenarios.

A named onsite Wisconsin Data Center transition cell will coordinate discovery, plant-sensitive dependencies, risk controls, data requests, and daily issue resolution. It will be backed by follow-the-sun HCLTech infrastructure, cloud, service-management, and security specialists so workshops and analysis do not wait on time zones. Our team is designed to integrate with Kohler and incumbent providers through a clear RACI, disciplined escalation, and transparent evidence reviews, giving Kohler both local accountability and global operating-model depth.

HCLTech is the right partner because our proposal starts where Kohler’s risk starts: continuity of manufacturing and enterprise services while strategic hosting options are evaluated. We are not asking Kohler to buy a predetermined cloud migration, DCaaS move, or managed-services transition. We are offering a fixed-price, vendor-neutral decision lab that makes the economics, architecture, security posture, operating model, and transition risk visible before commitments are made.

Our win themes are embedded in the commercial model and the work products. Evidence-led hybrid placement prevents binary decisions. Low-disruption sequencing, rollback gates, and the Wisconsin transition cell protect plants, enterprise platforms, hospitality, analytics, and digital workloads. Secure landing zones, identity integration, policy-as-code, region strategy, and hybrid connectivity establish the foundation for cloud and AI readiness. Follow-the-sun support, RACI discipline, and vendor coexistence address Kohler’s multi-partner reality. Open-book TCO, FinOps guardrails, SLA, uptime, MTTR, licensing, and chargeback/showback analysis create transparent business accountability.

The result is a leadership-ready Polaris package that Kohler owns, can defend, and can execute without losing control of cost, resilience, security, or operational continuity.

critical_issues: The claimed manufacturing deal-corpus evidence is not present in the raw data. DEAL-003, DEAL-089, DEAL-038, DEAL-094, DEAL-056, and DEAL-037 are unsupported and should not be used to justify win probability.

Most competitor intelligence is unsupported by the provided raw sources. The raw data contains no TCS, Accenture, Capgemini, or Infosys earnings releases, press releases, job postings, Wisconsin hiring evidence, or competitor-specific procurement awards.

The public contract price anchors cited for TCS, Accenture, Capgemini, and Infosys are not in the provided procurement data. The actual US federal procurement extract lists Lockheed Martin, SAIC, GDIT, Northrop Grumman, and Amentum awards, not the competitor anchors cited by the agents.
All normalized competitor bid ranges are based on unsupported operating-margin figures and assumed delivery cost. They should be treated as model-derived speculation, not evidence-based pricing.

The Evaluation Template Excel claim is only partially supportable. The raw data includes evaluation dimensions but does not directly confirm an external Excel template, its exact weights, or that it is unavailable except by absence from the supplied materials.

The 20% fee-at-risk manufacturing-continuity guarantee is not supported by the raw data. The knowledge base mentions a contractual transition-disruption warranty, but not a 20% fee holdback, application performance guarantee, or zero-disruption guarantee with those exact terms.

The assertion that none of the named competitors has recent Wisconsin Data Center hosting contracts or confirmed Wisconsin hiring is not directly verified. The supplied data simply does not contain such evidence.

warnings: The recommended price of $279,533 is directly verified by the Project Polaris knowledge-base record, but market competitiveness is not validated by public contract comparables or cloud pricing details in the provided raw data.
The direct Project Polaris record is a WIP proposal record, not a proven win. Win probability should therefore be reduced below the agent's 48% estimate.

The solution-fit claims are mostly supported by the Project Polaris proposal content, including hybrid hosting, DCaaS, retained data center, edge/plant hosting, open-book TCO, FinOps, resiliency, operating model, and transition sequencing.
